title: Custom detection vs ESCU baseline comparison
spl:
index=summary sourcetype=detection_metrics
| eval family=if(match(rule_name, "(?i)escu"), "ESCU", "Custom")
| timechart span=1d sum(hit_count) by family
description: Side-by-side hit volume and severity for custom rules vs ESCU-packaged searches.
value: Side-by-side hit volume and severity for custom rules vs ESCU-packaged searches.
implementation: Normalize field names to your Splunk ES version and index names. Use summary indexing or ES introspection where `detection_metrics` is not present. For YARA, deploy a scripted input or HEC to ingest scanner JSON.
knownFalsePositives: Side-by-side mismatches from different schedules, dev-only copies, or renamed rules can look worse than the real health of custom versus baseline content when time ranges or scopes do not line up.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security / custom.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Summary metrics keyed by rule_name.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Normalize field names to your Splunk ES version and index names. Use summary indexing or ES introspection where `detection_metrics` is not present. For YARA, deploy a scripted input or HEC to ingest scanner JSON.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=summary sourcetype=detection_metrics
| eval family=if(match(rule_name, "(?i)escu"), "ESCU", "Custom")
| timechart span=1d sum(hit_count) by family
```

Understanding this SPL

**Custom detection vs ESCU baseline comparison** — Side-by-side hit volume and severity for custom rules vs ESCU-packaged searches.

Documented **Data sources**: Summary metrics keyed by rule_name. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security / custom. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: summary; **sourcetype**: detection_metrics. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Detection type** for this use case: Operational metrics — interpret thresholds and fields in that context.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=summary, sourcetype=detection_metrics. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **family** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `timechart` plots the metric over time using **span=1d** buckets with a separate series **by family** — ideal for trending and alerting on this use case.


Step 3 — Validate
Pick one rule that exists in both custom and shipped form and **diff schedules, data scope, and naming** in Content Management so the comparison is really apples to apples.



Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table, Single value, Time chart

Scripted input (generic example)
This use case relies on a scripted input. In the app's local/inputs.conf add a stanza such as:

```ini
[script://$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/YourApp/bin/collect.sh]
interval = 300
sourcetype = your_sourcetype
index = main
disabled = 0
```

The script should print one event per line (e.g. key=value). Example minimal script (bash):

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Output metrics or events, one per line
echo "metric=value timestamp=$(date +%s)"
```

For full details (paths, scheduling, permissions), see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md
grandmaExplanation: We compare our home-built rules to the shipped baseline so we do not double-pay effort or miss what the community already fixed.
